 14â†’/**
 15â†’ * Defines a scope for new coroutines. Every **coroutine builder** (like [launch], [async], etc.)
 16â†’ * is an extension on [CoroutineScope] and inherits its [coroutineContext][CoroutineScope.coroutineContext]
 17â†’ * to automatically propagate all its elements and cancellation.
 18â†’ *
 19â†’ * The best ways to obtain a standalone instance of the scope are [CoroutineScope()] and [MainScope()] factory functions,
 20â†’ * taking care to cancel these coroutine scopes when they are no longer needed (see section on custom usage below for
 21â†’ * explanation and example).
 22â†’ *
 23â†’ * Additional context elements can be appended to the scope using the [plus][CoroutineScope.plus] operator.
 24â†’ *
 25â†’ * ### Convention for structured concurrency
 26â†’ *
 27â†’ * Manual implementation of this interface is not recommended, implementation by delegation should be preferred instead.
 28â†’ * By convention, the [context of a scope][CoroutineScope.coroutineContext] should contain an instance of a
 29â†’ * [job][Job] to enforce the discipline of **structured concurrency** with propagation of cancellation.
 30â†’ *
 31â†’ * Every coroutine builder (like [launch], [async], and others)
 32â†’ * and every scoping function (like [coroutineScope] and [withContext]) provides _its own_ scope
 33â†’ * with its own [Job] instance into the inner block of code it runs.
 34â†’ * By convention, they all wait for all the coroutines inside their block to complete before completing themselves,
 35â†’ * thus enforcing the structured concurrency. See [Job] documentation for more details.
 36â†’ *
 37â†’ * ### Android usage
 38â†’ *
 39â†’ * Android has first-party support for coroutine scope in all entities with the lifecycle.
 40â†’ * See [the corresponding documentation](https://developer.android.com/topic/libraries/architecture/coroutines#lifecyclescope).
 41â†’ *
 42â†’ * ### Custom usage
 43â†’ *
 44â†’ * `CoroutineScope` should be declared as a property on entities with a well-defined lifecycle that are
 45â†’ * responsible for launching child coroutines. The corresponding instance of `CoroutineScope` shall be created
 46â†’ * with either `CoroutineScope()` or `MainScope()`:
 47â†’ *
 48â†’ * * `CoroutineScope()` uses the [context][CoroutineContext] provided to it as a parameter for its coroutines 
 49â†’ *   and adds a [Job] if one is not provided as part of the context.
 50â†’ * * `MainScope()` uses [Dispatchers.Main] for its coroutines and has a [SupervisorJob].
 51â†’ *
 52â†’ * **The key part of custom usage of `CoroutineScope` is cancelling it at the end of the lifecycle.**
 53â†’ * The [CoroutineScope.cancel] extension function shall be used when the entity that was launching coroutines
 54â†’ * is no longer needed. It cancels all the coroutines that might still be running on behalf of it.
 55â†’ *
 56â†’ * For example:
 57â†’ *
 58â†’ * ```
 59â†’ * class MyUIClass {
 60â†’ *     val scope = MainScope() // the scope of MyUIClass, uses Dispatchers.Main
 61â†’ *
 62â†’ *     fun destroy() { // destroys an instance of MyUIClass
 63â†’ *         scope.cancel() // cancels all coroutines launched in this scope
 64â†’ *         // ... do the rest of cleanup here ...
 65â†’ *     }
 66â†’ *
 67â†’ *     /*
 68â†’ *      * Note: if this instance is destroyed or any of the launched coroutines
 69â†’ *      * in this method throws an exception, then all nested coroutines are cancelled.
 70â†’ *      */
 71â†’ *     fun showSomeData() = scope.launch { // launched in the main thread
 72â†’ *        // ... here we can use suspending functions or coroutine builders with other dispatchers
 73â†’ *        draw(data) // draw in the main thread
 74â†’ *     }
 75â†’ * }
 76â†’ * ```
 77â†’ */
 78â†’public interface CoroutineScope {
 79â†’    /**
 80â†’     * The context of this scope.
 81â†’     * Context is encapsulated by the scope and used for implementation of coroutine builders that are extensions on the scope.

 15â†’// --------------- core job interfaces ---------------
 16â†’
 17â†’/**
 18â†’ * A background job. Conceptually, a job is a cancellable thing with a life-cycle that
 19â†’ * culminates in its completion.
 20â†’ *
 21â†’ * Jobs can be arranged into parent-child hierarchies where cancellation
 22â†’ * of a parent leads to immediate cancellation of all its [children] recursively.
 23â†’ * Failure of a child with an exception other than [CancellationException] immediately cancels its parent and,
 24â†’ * consequently, all its other children. This behavior can be customized using [SupervisorJob].
 25â†’ *
 26â†’ * The most basic instances of `Job` interface are created like this:
 27â†’ *
 28â†’ * * **Coroutine job** is created with [launch][CoroutineScope.launch] coroutine builder.
 29â†’ *   It runs a specified block of code and completes on completion of this block.
 30â†’ * * **[CompletableJob]** is created with a `Job()` factory function.
 31â†’ *   It is completed by calling [CompletableJob.complete].
 32â†’ *
 33â†’ * Conceptually, an execution of a job does not produce a result value. Jobs are launched solely for their
 34â†’ * side effects. See [Deferred] interface for a job that produces a result.
 35â†’ *
 36â†’ * ### Job states
 37â†’ *
 38â†’ * A job has the following states:
 39â†’ *
 40â†’ * | **State**                        | [isActive] | [isCompleted] | [isCancelled] |
 41â†’ * | -------------------------------- | ---------- | ------------- | ------------- |
 42â†’ * | _New_ (optional initial state)   | `false`    | `false`       | `false`       |
 43â†’ * | _Active_ (default initial state) | `true`     | `false`       | `false`       |
 44â†’ * | _Completing_ (transient state)   | `true`     | `false`       | `false`       |
 45â†’ * | _Cancelling_ (transient state)   | `false`    | `false`       | `true`        |
 46â†’ * | _Cancelled_ (final state)        | `false`    | `true`        | `true`        |
 47â†’ * | _Completed_ (final state)        | `false`    | `true`        | `false`       |
 48â†’ *
 49â†’ * Usually, a job is created in the _active_ state (it is created and started). However, coroutine builders
 50â†’ * that provide an optional `start` parameter create a coroutine in the _new_ state when this parameter is set to
 51â†’ * [CoroutineStart.LAZY]. Such a job can be made _active_ by invoking [start] or [join].
 52â†’ *
 53â†’ * A job is _active_ while the coroutine is working or until [CompletableJob] is completed,
 54â†’ * or until it fails or cancelled.
 55â†’ *
 56â†’ * Failure of an _active_ job with an exception makes it _cancelling_.
 57â†’ * A job can be cancelled at any time with [cancel] function that forces it to transition to
 58â†’ * the _cancelling_ state immediately. The job becomes _cancelled_  when it finishes executing its work and
 59â†’ * all its children complete.
 60â†’ *
 61â†’ * Completion of an _active_ coroutine's body or a call to [CompletableJob.complete] transitions the job to
 62â†’ * the _completing_ state. It waits in the _completing_ state for all its children to complete before
 63â†’ * transitioning to the _completed_ state.
 64â†’ * Note that _completing_ state is purely internal to the job. For an outside observer a _completing_ job is still
 65â†’ * active, while internally it is waiting for its children.
 66â†’ *
 67â†’ * ```
 68â†’ *                                       wait children
 69â†’ * +-----+ start  +--------+ complete   +-------------+  finish  +-----------+
 70â†’ * | New | -----> | Active | ---------> | Completing  | -------> | Completed |
 71â†’ * +-----+        +--------+            +-------------+          +-----------+
 72â†’ *                  |  cancel / fail       |
 73â†’ *                  |     +----------------+
 74â†’ *                  |     |
 75â†’ *                  V     V
 76â†’ *              +------------+                           finish  +-----------+
 77â†’ *              | Cancelling | --------------------------------> | Cancelled |
 78â†’ *              +------------+                                   +-----------+
 79â†’ * ```
 80â†’ *
 81â†’ * A `Job` instance in the
 82â†’ * [coroutineContext](https://kotlinlang.org/api/latest/jvm/stdlib/kotlin.coroutines/coroutine-context.html)
 83â†’ * represents the coroutine itself.
 84â†’ *
 85â†’ * ### Cancellation cause
 86â†’ *
 87â†’ * A coroutine job is said to _complete exceptionally_ when its body throws an exception;
 88â†’ * a [CompletableJob] is completed exceptionally by calling [CompletableJob.completeExceptionally].
 89â†’ * An exceptionally completed job is cancelled and the corresponding exception becomes the _cancellation cause_ of the job.
 90â†’ *
 91â†’ * Normal cancellation of a job is distinguished from its failure by the type of this exception that caused its cancellation.
 92â†’ * A coroutine that threw [CancellationException] is considered to be _cancelled normally_.
 93â†’ * If a cancellation cause is a different exception type, then the job is considered to have _failed_.
 94â†’ * When a job has _failed_, then its parent gets cancelled with the exception of the same type,
 95â†’ * thus ensuring transparency in delegating parts of the job to its children.
 96â†’ *
 97â†’ * Note, that [cancel] function on a job only accepts [CancellationException] as a cancellation cause, thus
 98â†’ * calling [cancel] always results in a normal cancellation of a job, which does not lead to cancellation
 99â†’ * of its parent. This way, a parent can [cancel] its own children (cancelling all their children recursively, too)
100â†’ * without cancelling itself.
101â†’ *
102â†’ * ### Concurrency and synchronization
103â†’ *
104â†’ * All functions on this interface and on all interfaces derived from it are **thread-safe** and can
105â†’ * be safely invoked from concurrent coroutines without external synchronization.
106â†’ *
107â†’ * ### Not stable for inheritance
108â†’ *
109â†’ * **`Job` interface and all its derived interfaces are not stable for inheritance in 3rd party libraries**,
110â†’ * as new methods might be added to this interface in the future, but is stable for use.
111â†’ */
